html:
Gustav of Vasa (German: Gustav, Prince of Vasa), born as the Swedish Crown Prince on November 9, 1799, and died on August 5, 1877, in Pillnitz, was the son of King Gustav IV Adolf of Sweden and Queen Fredrika. He was Crown Prince from 1799 to 1809, and subsequently a claimant to the throne while living in exile. He was also an Austrian nobleman and held the rank of Field Marshal Lieutenant in the Austrian army.

During his studies starting in 1816, Gustav often used the title "Count of Itterburg." In Austria, from 1829 onwards, he used the title "Prince of Vasa" and was styled as Royal Highness.

== The Regency Period == Gustav of Vasa was the only son of Gustav IV Adolf, and also the grandson of Gustav III. Gustav was a sickly child, and "his development was very delayed," according to Adlerbeth: "Even at the age of two, he could barely speak." As a young child, he contracted whooping cough and developed a curvature of the spine. Esaias Tegnér, who lived at Ulriksdal during the summer of 1803, wrote in a letter that he "almost daily had the invaluable pleasure of seeing the noble and gracious royal children, among whom was Sweden's hope. It is only regrettable that he limps so terribly!" Gustav was nine years old when his father, Gustav IV Adolf, was overthrown on March 13, 1809, in a military-led coup. Initially, he shared the imprisonment of his parents at Gripsholm Castle, but in December 1809, he was smuggled out of the country by Colonel Posse. However, this expulsion was not the worst thing that could have happened to the prince. According to preserved letters written by Hans Gabriel Trolle-Wachtmeister, there were plans to assassinate Prince Gustav in order to eliminate a threat to the new ruling power.

When the heir apparent, Karl August, suddenly died in 1810, it raised hopes for the deposed Gustav IV Adolf that his eldest son might become a candidate. Many had plans for him. The Danes dreamed of a marriage between him and a Danish princess, who together would rule over a future united Scandinavia. The "Gustavian party," led by Jakob De la Gardie, unsuccessfully tried in 1809 and 1810 to propose him as heir after Karl XIII (1748–1818). In 1816, his mother bought the castle ruins of Itterburg, and from that year on, Gustav called himself "Count of Itterburg" in incognito.

His son, Gustav, was first referred to as a prince of Sweden among politicians in Europe. He would be mentioned frequently. Napoleon believed that the Swedes had treated their king poorly and would have liked to see the son ascend the Swedish throne. At the Congress of Vienna in 1815, Gustav's case was argued by Sir Sidney Smith, who had previously served under Gustav III. The Russian Tsar Alexander I also supported him. Karl XIII's adopted heir, Prince Carl Johan (born Jean Bernadotte), protested. However, other and more important decisions needed to be made during the congress, so the matter died down. But Carl Johan was in Stockholm drafting a new law that stipulated the death penalty for anyone within Sweden's borders who mentioned Prince Gustav. [Source needed]

On a return trip from an educational journey to Italy, Gustav applied to the Emperor of Austria to join the Austrian army and was accepted. He was 24 years old at the time and had a solid education. One day, he received an invitation from the King of the Netherlands, William I, to visit him in order to meet his young daughter. They became engaged, but the announcement mistakenly referred to the groom as the Prince of Sweden. The result was that Carl Johan in Stockholm declared that he was prepared to demand redress with arms for the insult to Swedish honor. "Does Sweden have greater concerns?" asked the Austrian Foreign Minister. The major powers of Europe divided into opposing camps, bribes were paid, Gustav was stripped of his right to call himself a Swedish prince, but then the Emperor of Austria made him a "Prince of Vasa."

During the Greek War of Independence (1821–1829), rumors circulated that Gustav would be put forward as a candidate for the Greek throne, which never happened.

On his thirty-first birthday in 1830, Prince Gustav married his nineteen-year-old cousin, Louise of Baden. Napoleon's son, who was sent to his grandfather's court in Vienna after his father's final defeat and there called the Duke of Reichstadt, attended the wedding lunch. Gustav received his own regiment (Kaiserliche königliche infanterieregimente Prinz von Wasa Nr. 60), and the Duke of Reichstadt became one of his commanders. The marriage was unhappy. He accused her of infidelity. Financial problems also seemed to exacerbate the relationship between the couple, and the marriage ended in divorce in 1843.

Afterward, Prince Gustav became a restless traveler, just like his father had been. As a divorced man, he visited almost all the countries of Europe, mostly as a guest of some princely relative or esteemed acquaintance. Nevertheless, he had more interests than most: everything from hunting and riding to architecture, theater, literature, and botany.

The only time he visited Sweden after 1809 was during a trip to Denmark in 1862, when he stayed for a day as an unknown tourist in Helsingborg and Ramlösa.

He died in the summer of 1877, after three months of illness, at his daughter Carola of Saxony's home, Pillnitz Castle in Saxony, and was buried there.

How certain are we about this?

Information about people from the 19th century is very well documented (through census records, newspapers, and letters). The uncertainty mainly concerns selection and perspective – whose stories are highlighted.
tldr: Gustav of Vasa was a Swedish prince who lost his claim to the throne after his father was overthrown in a coup d'état in 1809. He spent the rest of his life abroad, primarily in Austria, where he became a nobleman and served in the military.
lattlast:
Gustav was born as the Crown Prince but never became king. When his father, King Gustav IV Adolf, was overthrown in a coup in 1809, the young prince was forced to leave Sweden. There were plans to kill him because he was considered a threat to the new regime, but he survived and moved abroad.

In Austria, he received a new title as Prince of Vasa and became an officer in the Austrian army. He married his cousin Louise of Baden in 1830, but the marriage did not work out, and they divorced after a few years. After the divorce, he traveled extensively throughout Europe and developed interests in hunting, architecture, and plants.

Gustav only visited Sweden once after leaving the country, and that was as an anonymous tourist in 1862. He died in Germany in 1877, having lived for many years outside of Swedish politics.
